questions.text: Which Go library is recommended for building new Terraform providers?
questions.explanation: The Terraform Plugin Framework is the recommended library for new providers. It offers better typing with Go generics, improved diagnostics with path-aware errors, native support for protocol version 6, and better alignment with modern Terraform features. SDKv2 still works but is in maintenance mode.

questions.text: What file configures local provider development overrides?
questions.explanation: The ~/.terraformrc (or %APPDATA%/terraform.rc on Windows) file can contain provider_installation blocks with dev_overrides to point to locally built provider binaries during development. This bypasses the registry and lock file for the specified provider.

questions.text: What are the four CRUD operations a Terraform resource must implement?
questions.explanation: Every Terraform resource implements Create (called on first apply), Read (called during refresh/plan to detect drift), Update (called when attributes change), and Delete (called on destroy). Some resources are immutable — they implement Create/Delete but not Update (they force replacement).

questions.text: What is 'terraform providers schema -json' useful for?

options.text: Exporting full schema of all providers and resources for tooling and IDE support

questions.explanation: terraform providers schema -json outputs the complete schema for all configured providers in JSON format. This is used by IDE extensions (like the HashiCorp Terraform VS Code extension) for autocomplete and validation, and by tooling that generates documentation or performs static analysis.

options.text: Passes module-specific metadata from modules to providers

questions.explanation: provider_meta blocks in a module allow passing module-specific metadata to providers. This is used for tracking which module a resource came from — useful for billing attribution and telemetry in provider analytics. Providers must explicitly support provider_meta schemas.

questions.text: What protocol version do Terraform Plugin Framework providers use?
questions.explanation: Terraform Plugin Framework uses the gRPC-based Protocol v6, which supports newer Terraform features. SDKv2 uses Protocol v5. Both protocols work with Terraform 1.x, but Protocol v6 enables features like write-only attributes and other modern improvements.

questions.text: How do you publish a custom provider to the Terraform Registry?

options.text: Push to GitHub with a release tag and connect the repository to registry.terraform.io
Submit a pull request to the Terraform providers repository

questions.explanation: Publishing to the Terraform Registry involves: creating a GitHub repository named terraform-provider-<name>, signing release binaries with GPG, tagging releases (v1.0.0), and registering on registry.terraform.io linked to your GitHub account. The registry automatically indexes releases from the repo.

options.text: Combining multiple providers into one binary (SDKv2 + Framework)

questions.explanation: terraform-plugin-muxserver allows combining resources implemented with SDKv2 and resources implemented with the Plugin Framework into a single provider binary. This enables gradual migration from SDKv2 to the Framework without rewriting all resources at once.

questions.text: What is a 'write-only attribute' in Terraform Plugin Framework?

options.text: An attribute that can only be set in the provider block
An attribute that is sent to the API but never stored in state
An attribute that cannot be changed after initial creation
An attribute that only appears in plan output

questions.explanation: Write-only attributes (a new Plugin Framework feature) are sent to the provider during create/update but are never stored in state. This is ideal for secrets like passwords — the value is used once but not persisted in the potentially less-secure state file.

questions.text: What does the 'ImportState' method on a resource implement?

options.text: Batch importing multiple resources at once
Populating state from an existing resource given only its ID (supports terraform import)
Importing resource schema from an external API

questions.explanation: ImportState allows users to run 'terraform import' on a resource. Given only the resource's ID (from the real API), ImportState populates the Terraform state. After import, Terraform runs Read to refresh all attributes. Without ImportState, the resource cannot be imported.
